text: High intensity interval training increases VO2max by an average of 0.51 L/min in healthy sedentary to recreationally active adults over 6-13 weeks of training.
Longer high-intensity intervals produce larger VO2max gains of approximately 0.8-0.9 L/min, with evidence of a marked response in all subjects.
Interval training produces substantially larger VO2max improvements (up to ~1.0 L/min) compared to the modest changes typically seen with continuous endurance training alone.
Effective high-intensity interval training protocols require at least 3 sessions per week, at least 10 minutes of high-intensity work per session, and a minimum 1:1 work-to-rest ratio.
Combined interval and continuous training programs can produce VO2max gains comparable to or exceeding interval-only programs, suggesting both modalities complement each other.

The perception that VO2max has limited trainability may be an artifact of relying on moderate-intensity continuous training; interval training demonstrates that VO2max is more trainable than previously assumed.
